codeReview.summary: The feature adds a 1MB body-limit middleware and route-chain integration, but the oversized-body error path can emit two JSON payloads in one response, producing an invalid JSON body for 413 responses on the MaxBytesReader path.

issues.description: In bodyLimitResponseWriter.WriteHeader, calling WriteAPIError on the underlying writer during 4xx override writes a full JSON response immediately, but the handler's original WriteAPIError call continues and encodes another JSON object via the wrapped writer. Because WriteAPIError writes both header and body (internal/shared/httputil/httputil.go:31-32), oversized requests can return concatenated JSON documents, violating the requirement for an informative JSON error body.
413-body assertions decode only the first JSON object and do not assert EOF, so tests can pass even when multiple JSON objects are concatenated in the response body.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The worker marked skillFeedback.followedProcedure=true, but transcript order shows implementation landed before RED-phase tests, conflicting with security-backend-worker guidance to write failing tests first.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript skeleton shows Create/Edit middleware steps before Create internal/rpc/bodylimit_security_test.go and before first TestBodyLimit run.

sharedStateObservations.observation: There is no shared guidance documenting that response-writer interception around WriteAPIError can double-write bodies; this likely contributed to a subtle invalid-JSON regression.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Implementation in internal/shared/httputil/bodylimit.go uses WriteAPIError inside WriteHeader interception, while httputil.WriteAPIError itself always writes body after WriteHeader (internal/shared/httputil/httputil.go:31-32).

summary: Review failed: commit 6937205d introduces a blocking response-format bug in the oversized-body path. The middleware can emit two JSON objects in one 413 response, and current tests do not detect this because they only decode the first object.
